Bridging the Gap

"Every system is perfectly designed to achieve the results it achieves" - Donald Berwick

Valleys

The average amount of time it takes for a scientific discovery to reach clinical practice is 17 years. Why is this? The reasons are complex, but even when a treatment shows promise, even when it succeeds it human trials, it has to cross what clinical trialists refer to as a' valley of death'. This particular valley is adoption of treatment by practitioners.

You wouldn't be faulted for thinking that once a treatment has been proven to be effective, that adoption should be simple. Unfortunately, our systems of care are rarely designed to rapidly implement new interventions effectively. Even when they do adopt new interventions, it can still be incredibly difficult for patients to connect with the treatments they need because our healthcare systems are often exceptionally difficult to navigate.

So, what do we do? Do our best, make modest adjustments where we can, and continue operating within a broken system or commit to fundamentally changing the way that care is delivered and start designing a system that can meet the actual needs of both providers and patients.

Building and crossing bridges

As Director of Care Redesign for the Brain and Behavior Innovation Center (BBICEN), Dr. Love takes on projects which are focused the logistics of mental health care delivery. She focuses on how to effectively move treatments out of the lab and into the clinic, ensuring they actually reach the patients they were created for and do so at scale. Her work aims to accelerate healthcare transformation by uncovering smarter, more efficient ways to optimize how mental health services are actually delivered.
